transcript.transcript: Oh no, no pounding. I didn't know that. No, because Michael said it was daddy then. He's not a good boy. I'm not phone. Well, I'm a microphone. Is that a phone? Yeah. But don't you know that kid for a... How long did it take you to go to Spokane when you... Well, to tell the truth, we never went. I didn't know what Spokane was until his dad got 1923, 1923. 1924. No, no. Dodge? He got the Dodge in 1923. Oh. When he bought the Dodge car, one of them open touring cars, what they called them. I never run it. I don't know to this day how to run it. Nothing. Never even, I did two, eight or so. My dad wanted me to learn how to drive. Oh, that was the Uncle Phil. Was that Model A Ford? After my dad died. You kind of went through the gate then. Well, no, Johnny, that was our, come here, our, the car that, yeah, I think in them days, I don't remember Mike, I don't know, but they said a couple hours, wasn't it Johnny? Couple hours or longer, you know, to make the trip into Spokane and, you know. One way. One way, yeah. Now, that's what I don't know because I never, I never been there. Do you remember when they built the bridge across That old town there? Used to go on to cross the ferry. Uh-huh. His dad and I, we did, you know, one time we came from Spokane or going, I don't know which. Well, you bet you had to, you know, bleh. Well, we had the ferry, the Thema Ferry also, too. I remember crossing that one time. I don't know, we went someplace. Your dad was still living too that time. I know. The Thema, Thema ferry. Carriage ferry, we used to call it them days. Oh yeah. And then there was one in the cleave. That was it. Oh, I remember when the bridge was built. Newport and the one across our, you know, the pondway here too. Oh, we had a ferry here too, also, you know. Here in Priest River to cross. The people across the river are what's going to do. They had to, you know, and that was as a young. Yeah, they built that in 1919, was it? The bridge? Uh-huh. 1918? 19... Well, I don't know who said, I can give you a definite, I don't remember, you mean the bridge? The bridge across Pondray here? What year, John? That I don't remember. Well, I can't remember. No, I tell you. No, I think it was later than that, I think. I don't know if your dad was living when they built that bridge. He died the year of the 26th. I don't think Johnny's that... Well, maybe they just had the ferry down below and then found the ferry at Seymour. Well, they did. That's right, they had. And one in Laclede, you remember that? Yeah, one in Laclede. The one at Thema is the one that you take to go to Oak Falls, and is that the word? Oh, you could go. Yeah, John, you could take that route. Well, on the other side. You can go right up here, right here to the Thamer Hill, and they used to be there where needs lived, you know, where needs lived there. And before the highway was put in there, then you just cut down and take the ferry across there. Then you'd come out on the south side of the river, the same way at Lake Fleet, you know, where the ferry was there at Lake Fleet. And then they had the one here at St. Mark's, and the one at Newport. The one in Newport, yeah. Then they had another one there at, around by Puget. Oh, well, I've never been there to know, but anyhow, you see, yeah. I understand you used to go to the Attic. I hope to tell you. Well, the early part, the old early days, Grandma and Grandpa, if they come, you betcha, a family, you know, of five, but then the mother and the dad, and five children. Oh yeah, I had met them all. Molly was one of the daughters, Louise was another one, Annie was another one, and Alexander, the one of the brothers, and Biel was two boys and the three sisters in that one family. There, them there, they were pretty much, you know, the steady. Oh, yeah. She used to churn for me, the mother. Churn the butter. Churn the butter. We had a 16-gallon barrel, the oak barrel, you know. You know, you had to make sure you fasten up your lid good, you know, slant it good, or you'd be done. Oh yeah. She used to come, you know, she liked to, you know, churn, motioning, you know. Oh yes, oh yes. They were very, very, very, very friendly, very nice people, just the same. Were they Calisto or Cusick? Well, believe it or not, Mike, I think they were down below here. What is it? I don't know. The Colville Reservation. Probably Callisto. Down here, down around the Newport, I think. That's where they, you know. Yeah. Because they come with their horses, you know, and then set up their sheep, you know, and... But when Grandma and Grandpa first settled there, oh yeah, yeah, they... I remember Grandma telling us that they... I remember grandma telling us, now they had Henry Tigers, our oldest of the, you know, grandpa and grandma. And Annie Lawson now, she's still living, she's 90-some years old, in a nursing home, poor thing. And then that's kid dad. The three of them, you know, and grandma used to tell us that If a white man would come, the kids would go and hide. This was his uncle and aunt, you know. His dad wasn't yet born, and was much younger than the other two. And so she ate with the Indians, because the Indians was with them around all the time. No kidding. Grandma used to tell us that. Oh yeah. So that was it. That was my end. Were the Tejes, At this time, they were using canvas tent type material. I think it was canvas at the seam material. Mike, I can remember going down there, and I guess this is horrible, because Dad was really an outdoorsman. He loved to hunt, and he loved to fish. And I can remember going down there with him below the railroad track right now. Oh, that's where they used to catch the fish. And they used to be as high as 12 of those tepees. And the inside of them were, well, maybe half as big as this room. You got to sit in there and they got a fire right in the middle and the smoke always seemed to go right on up, right out through them. Oh dear goodness, anyhow. And I'll never forget it. Oh well, sure enough, they used to come. All the time. They used to play baseball, you know, all along. Even a lot of the, you know, if there was neighbors around, they would say, you know, yeah, so she ate very much, very good. Yeah, and they used to dry their fish, too, you know, in the teepees, I think they used to smoke them. Oh sure. Dad, I can remember him hanging them dry up there in the attic up there in the old house. Up there, up home in our house, up home. Just the raw fish, saw them and then hang them up and dry them and you take them out and seal them off, put them in a little oil and that's where they agreed. He learned that from me. There was chars there in that river. I don't know if there, you know, all good red bellies, white fish, everything. The sand needs to come up. No, no sand. I call them red bellies, that's a big trout, you know. I don't know what Johnny calls them. I used to catch them by the house of the architects. I don't know what's in a house, Johnny. I don't know what you call them, but in a house. Oh yeah. They're big, good, nice, nice ones. I got a few this winter. That's what she said. I still got one of Dad's fishbowl terries, believe it or not, and that was the same day that we went for a ride. You still got it? We went for a ride when he bought that brand new Dodge, when the first bridge that was put across the Dickens Heat, and that was a, that was a stand bridge, you know, where the bridge is now. And that was a stand bridge, and he crossed it on plank. Yeah, he made us all get out. He made us all get out. Yeah, and that's something. Yeah, we walked across, Mom and the four of us kids, walked across that and the same day he bought this pole. That's your birthday, I think. He bought it down here at Huffman's. Yeah, that's right. Huffman's first. I don't know what he paid for it, but I kept it. Six dollars. I remember that, John. Six dollars, six ninety-five, and I've got the tip to it. And I kind of, just a, Just a keepsake is what it is, you know, a hand split bamboo rod. Oh yeah. And that's the same one that he had in 1923 or when he bought that dog. Yeah. The same one. Yeah, yeah. Well, that's it I guess. You know. When you used to churn butter, what was the procedure for making the butter? How'd you go about it? Well, I churned it, you know, you know when it's done, because it's, you know, mixed, you know, like a, you know, big rumbling. And then we had a huge big hardwood bowl. You know, I still got the spoon of that by gum, come to think about it, if I'm not sure to fill. You hear what I said? I said I'm not sure to fill. I still got the pound, the pound little frame, what do you call it, to make the pound. Oh, the precedent? I don't know what the dickens is. A mole? No, it's a mole, a little mole, a little pound, but one pound, one pound exactly. That was, you know, and then this, and this big, huge, big hardwood bowl, you know, the night before, you know, when I know I had to turn the next day, all the skaters, you know, out of the, you know, pantry or, you know, call it, And soak it overnight, you know, I had a little, you know, kind of a little frack, you know, so I had to, you know, see to it, you know, that it would go together, you know. And that is, yeah. And then wash the butter in there, you know, and wash and wash and wash and got it, you know. Yeah, and then after you know that the buttermilk was all out of it, you know, with that wooden spoon. I got it, I told you. It wasn't so easy to work. Now I couldn't do beans. My hands. But... You're pretty good yet, Mike. Well, I got to be thankful at that because after all, you know, I was really there for a while. I didn't know if I was... But, okay. Let's leave it in the good Lord, you know, will look after us. I always say. Q. I can sing hard. A. Oh, and then you solved it, see, whatever. I really... I'm one to say that I never measure outside of medicine. That's something that's obey orders. You know what I mean? If I've got to take medicine, you better do what's got to do. But otherwise, for the cooking parts, you know, I don't say a teaspoon or a half a spoon or just judge, you know, salting, you know, or... And I used to have to watch, you know, how much salt, you know, you would, you know, put in. I had to put salt in it. And work with that spoon, boy, I tell you. And then mold it out, you know. That's where it comes, you know, with that big ice box that we had, you know, with a block of ice, it was in the summer months, you know. Yeah. And then set it, you know, on a towel, you know, with a block of ice, you know, and then set your butter. Until then, the next day, you know, let it go down, because we had, you know, to take it to the stores. Oh, yeah. But you had a 16-gallon churn. That was a 16-gallon churn. About how many pounds? No, no, this was a real churning. Ferguson, Jr. Oh, you had a little glass? No, no, no, this was a sixteen-gallon churner used to make butter in Johnny. Oh, no. Oh, we had one that was small. Ferguson, Jr. We had a small one, too. Oh, no, no, a small one, yeah, a little wooden. That was cute, that little, but become of it. We had another one, you know, with the Gramerkeiser had that, you know, crock, you know, maybe two gallons, and it had a wooden inside, yeah, whatever, and pum, pum, pum, okay. No kidding. And then we had a tiny glass one. What all that, I don't know. How many pounds did you make out of the big one? About 16 or 17, 18 pounds at one time. We had a separator, a big U.S. What was to make a separator. And turned, you know, to, you know, to, you don't, I don't think you, do you kids ever remember that big separator? Oh, the separator, I remember that. I remember the churn, too. You should, but everything was dumped out. You know, it's really, I can begin to think, how come? Got a cider mill we had? I still got the inside of the cider mill. You got that much, okay. The press? No. The buckets. I got the, no, the buckets are gone, but I've got the core, the two cores, you know, the heavy wheels. I'll tell you the truth about it. I turned that handle off too with this help grinding. We used to have apples by the tongue. You remember what Ben Davis are? That's the name of it. I can't tell you what you would call them today. Huge big trees, you know, they're bare. Oh my goodness, my goodness. They weren't much for cooking. They were much for eating. I don't know how come the poor, you know, the landlords, you know, they planted them, you know, they were big, they were huge trees. And that's what they're good for, to squeeze the juice out. Yeah. Yeah, I can remember 350 gallon barrels of cider down there every year, never missed. And I never used to drink a drop of it. She still don't. I don't know, but given the way... Also, Grandma Keyser used to drink it. Yes, Grandma, Grandma used to... All the people that would come, in them days, they'd all get together on, I can remember that, they'd go from one house on one weekend to the next house on another weekend, and they always had cider, wine, they made everything. They never bought anything, They made everything. Made because that's the only way you could get it. That's the only way you could get it. They never had no liquor, wasn't even legalized. The sugar was cheap at them days. I remember when I'd done my big share of canning, you'd get a hundred pounds back for four or seventy-five for less than five dollars. And that's why you could just dig in, you know, and can and jell and make and jam. But, you know, during the war, during the war, like, World War I, I remember that sugar went up to thirty-two dollars and a hundred pounds. And you had to pay a check. Now I remember that. I remember your dad sacked up a lot of... Atencio Your dad sacked up a lot of potatoes we had down in the basement, you know, to the, brought it here to the, and, and paid him in, you know, for a, for a, you know, get a sack of kidney. Sure. Did you raise a lot of potatoes on the plate? Oh, anything, everything. We had, just, raised everything. I raised a cauliflower, a cabbage, even eggplant. The eggplant just were to try it, just to see if it could be. Watermelon, used to call them little round ones, ice cream, that's what you call them today, I don't know. But, oh yeah, watermelon, we got grape vines there, you know, three different kind of grapes. We raised really everything. Salary, you know, it's work to raise salary though. At the old days, that's the way they used to work it. Little bits of little plants, you know, and then you set them, you know, far apart. Then as they grow up, you know, you have to seal them up with dirt, you know, make a trench, you know. But this is... But we work to that. I don't really sell it too much. No, but anything else, we used to give a lot of stuff away, which I'm glad today there's people to this day that they remember it, yes, and they bring that up. Lots of times they come to visit, you know, and they remember it, and I'm very much surprised about that. But the bond that I've ever had, why not? I stopped on the ground, a family that's glad to get him, why not? Good enough. So, is he doing something? Be careful for his head, Johnny. No, that's hard. That's what I say. It's really a... It's okay, It's fine nowadays, but I say, boy, if that would ever, ever, would, no, never, never, I'll never come back to anything that's, that's anymore, you know, I mean, to start out like that, no, I hope not. That wasn't the good old days? Well, everybody says the good old days, well, okay, they were good as much as we, you know. But if anybody would have to start out on them kind of a good old days, I don't know how far they'd get. That's very true. That's a good life. Most of them now do. They've got to start out with everything electric and everything. Yeah, but you know... Wash machines and everything. I think it's okay, though. You know, when we had the house redone up there, you know where the house is? The year of the 53, You know, we had to sow some property across the track for the dam building here. Okay, I put in, we get the house fixed and everything, and I was against it. Robert, he went ahead and he bought all the appliances on his own, see, because not me for any electrical appliances. The stove, you remember, I said, I argued with Robert, I said, I don't want no electric stove. Well, ever since I knew how to handle a piece of wood to stick in the, in the stove, which I handled plenty of it, winter months especially. Yeah, I wasn't gonna, I didn't feel like starting out with electric, but okay, it's good. No kidding. Well in the 50s it was a little bit different too, everything started to modernize. You know the first degree when we had... The depression was getting over with. Well, I sure didn't, I was really, In them late 20s, man alive, I... I can remember when Hoover was elected, 1928. And then in 1932, the rest were going to school. I was, I quit school and went to work. And I worked for $28.75 a month. Well, that's 50 cents a day. Frank Ansemow used to pay. Frank, you know Frank the Ansemow. I worked for him too, but there were two weeks on and two weeks off. A lot of people, you know, you got to have the young kids now, Mike, it's pretty hard to understand. Now I'll tell you, your grandma and grandpa, see our house was for the little gathering of people that was here in this town. No kidding, Grandma used to tell us that. They'd come on what's called a hen car. They used to board in the car from the section. And they'd bring up a keg of beer. No kidding now, I've sold that, and that's before my god's ears. Mr. One Cannon, he was president of one of the San Joaquin banks years ago. He was just a young man, he was working for the San Jose, And he joined the crowd from Priest River on the Hankar. And then they'd come as far as the crossing, of course, and then pull up the Hankar, you know. And then they, I don't know, somebody was there with a wheelbarrow and bring up the keg of beer up to that same house, the same house up there. But he, yeah, he remembered that. He was there with Uncle Phil one time, Mr. One Cannon, on the way, I don't know how come, he stopped there. And he mentioned that same house. He said, you know, I dance, you know, he says, my young days, they are home and our place, their kitchen. Kitchen is a big... Yeah, and he was, you know, president of one of the banks and... He was at that one bank. Yeah. Mr. Juan Cannon. He worked for 50 cents a day, that man. And how many others? But they made it. He was a nice person, Mr. Juan Cannon. Have you ever heard of his name? A. I'm sure you must have, because he lived in the – his home was in Sandpoint. I wonder what happened to his daughter. I forgot her name. King? Q. Yeah. A. I think she was married. His name was – he was working at the one of the standpoint banks through then, you know, naturally, his father-in-law. Yeah. Is he all right? I don't know if he's in there. When you cooked on wood, so did you use different kinds of wood to get your different kinds of heat? Miller-Well, wood was really, because we had fir up home on our place there, or tamarack. They don't, anything would come along, Johnny. Of course, like, you know, used to season the, you know, the wood, you know, because there wasn't too much up dry wood then, you know. My time, you know, when I, you know, lived there when Dad and I got married. But you cut the wood green in the spring of the year, you know, and then let it, you know, through the summer, my season, you know, brick wood, you know, and then bring it in the fall, September month, and throw it in one of the sheds as it was there. Oh yeah. Wood, wood, wood. But in the... I don't know, maybe. The winter months. I used to set up, because they don't remember it, I don't think. Because... You have a fire door. We were up home, you know, myself and them, you know, four of them. And when it's blizzering, now you could hear the howling winds up there when it was winter months. I never went to bed there. I never went to bed for... You slept in the chair. Right. I kept both stoves going. Kitchen stove, we had a lot of wood. We had to, you know, all it got, we used to put wood out on chairs, you know, people, you know, that way, because they were, you know, too small to cut wood themselves. But we had plenty of wood, I must say that. Well, of course, we had the wooded area, you know. Oh yeah. But to keep the two fires going for maybe two or three weeks at a time, winter months, I bet you. But you know, we had the house redone, I think, in the year of the 53, the siding and whatever, the insulation. I don't know what it was, but okay, leave it at that. Because that house was built in 1895. A great house. Yes. But, you know, I don't think the poor souls of them didn't know what the insulation was. Never had it. Well, whatever. And that was our, one was our house up there, no kidding. So then We had siding put on and the insulation in between. And so we wanted some roofs. I don't know how many different types of roofs for that house. Well, that's good many years, you know. That that house, That was the year your dad was born, your grandma and us. He was born, they were, he was born in Naples, 3rd of April, 1895. And they moved in, because they, just, just help, you know, no carpenters. My old name's Charlie Johnson, I think. He was a head carpenter. I don't think you ever heard of his name. You know, they owned this house, this brick house, when you go, you know, before you leave town, to your right, if you're going to Sand Point, right there before, on Main Street, at the corner house that used to belong to Mr. And Mrs. Johnson. Well, he knew carpentry. He was a... Johnny, do you remember him? Oh, sure. Huh? He used to go up there when they did up there at the Blue Lake. Yeah. That's the house that's right across the street from where the paper used to be. Yes, yes, yes, yes. Now. On the hill, right in the corner. Yeah, that was there, you know, and he was a head carpenter up home in the warehouse they built. I'm opening the door. He don't get interested. I'm going to open the door. You be a good boy now, or else Papa would have to thank you. Yeah, and that's, you know, good many years ago, but it's, you know, We had a pretty, pretty, well, you know, had a year of the 53, you know, as I said, we got, you know, a little, I had money we sold, you know, 73 acres, you know, across that track. That was for the... For the dam. Albany dam. Yeah. So we put in the, get the house a little more, you know, comfortable, you know. Had a bathroom. Otherwise we never had no bathroom in there. Boo! Go outside in the winter months. One or the other one. The kids all the time. I say, well, come in with one pretty soon, the other one I have to go to. I have to have the lantern lit up all the time. Nope. But, okay, we made it. I was just telling Mike, maybe I should have given that kind of idea. I said that we had outside toilets. I said, in the winter months, I said, one go in, one go out. Okay, before I come in with one and the other one has to go out. Oh, shoot, anyhow. They had to have a shovel handy. Shovel the path to go to it, because otherwise... Oh, shooy anyhow. Yeah, but it was, you know, That's the way it was done. And we did it. We done it to everybody around, the whole country around. No kidding. Did you have a well for water? Yes, we did. Lots of water. Yeah. We had the same well today. No problem with the water. Oh, yeah. We had a windmill there when I first, you know, did that, but then later on he dismantled the windmill and put in a gasoline engine to pump. It had a big, he built, yeah, I believe, yeah, in 1915, I think it's the name, it says the year he's building that there, a reservoir in the 1950s, built a huge big reservoir up, you know, on the hillside. There's a lot of pipeline in there from that well clear up there and then down to the barns then and to the house. No kidding. The barn is still there. No, the reservoir is still there. Well, the barn is still there, too. Oh, yeah. Yeah. And then, of course, then when we got the electricity installed, then Robert went ahead and he bought the electric pump, see, dismantled the gasoline engine and all of that. And he got, he bought one that pumped direct, direct from the well right to the house, see. And hide it under the... Electric, yeah. So that's the way it's set up, it's there now. So it's all abandoned now, all that long pipeline, all galvanized pipes too. They were all galvanized good pipes, you know, they were put on, you know. Yeah, but they're still there buried in the ground, yep, no kidding. Did they ever freeze up on you in the wintertime? Well, by gum, we had some, when you cross, you know, you know, right, you know, when you go to fields now, you know, right there, you know, I'm going to go with a, whatever, yeah, they used to freeze up once in a while, didn't they? Yeah. The pipes, the winter months. Well, I've done a lot of water packing, too. When you first started, you had to pack it? Well, no, when, they had a windmill there already, yeah, going, you know, you had to wait for the wind to blow before you could get the water coming. That was the set up at that. But then, you know, but yeah, we had a pet. No kidding. He's not doing anything though. He's okay. Oh yeah, you'll be good. Because then Mike's going to tell your daddy, I don't know about Jared, but he was a good boy though. Yeah. He's okay. Just so you don't make any noise or else Mike will put your... Where? Oh, your papa maybe. Okay? Yeah, well that's okay, that's alright. Now this here, Mike, now this is whatever you're working on. Now that'll be in the, are we going to get any copies of it? I'll tell you, I can make a copy of it for you, and I'll give it to you. And then it's going to go into the library down at the college, in the tape library. And then a copy of it will probably go in the Bonner's... Bonner's... Bonner's Ferry. ...Bonner County Historical Society. Uh-huh. You know, They already have your biography down there. I got a card here back then to come and attend one time. I still got the card, but I never once ever, you know, I'm not too much of jumping the fence right now to go anywhere. That's a scrub of a lot of it. But I, you know, could have, should have went up. You know, Ruth could have took me up, Gloria, somebody. But I didn't, really. Now, Grandpa Kaiser, he's got something in the review building in Spokane. Now, this wouldn't go into Spokane. No, no, no. Just about a copy. No, this is in the library at the NIC, so we have all the copy of the history. Yeah, that's good. Well, anyhow. There's so many things that I could think about that she didn't. But if I just... Well? But I don't know what more. I mean, she's been here a long time, more than a decade. 1900, maybe? And I was only six years old. Now, I wonder how the grandchildren or something, if they want to go somewhere, stay two, three nights like little John. It bothered me, no kidding. Because, and then I. Because I'm an animal.

lines.line: # Introduction and Early Conversations
> The conversation begins with some light and informal dialogue between the participants, setting a casual tone for the interview.
** introduction, informal, dialogue **

lines.line: # Family and Travel Memories

> Recollections of travel to Spokane and family anecdotes, including a mention of a Dodge car bought in 1923, are shared. The difficulties of travel and experiences with early automobiles are discussed.
** Spokane, travel, family, Dodge car, 1923, automobiles **
keywords: ** Spokane, travel, family, Dodge car, 1923, automobiles **

lines.line: # Bridges and Ferries
> Discussion about the construction of bridges and the use of ferries in the early 20th century, including the development of infrastructure in the region.
** bridges, ferries, infrastructure **

lines.line: # Fishing and Hunting Traditions
> The participants share memories of fishing and hunting, describing practices such as drying fish and the types of fish common in their area.
** fishing, hunting, traditions, fish drying **

** Spokane, travel, family, Dodge car, 1923, automobiles **
